title: The Somatic Practice of Receiving Money
body:
The Somatic Practice of Receiving Money

You're sitting there, maybe at your kitchen table, maybe at your desk. An invoice just landed in your inbox, or a client just confirmed payment. Perhaps you just checked your bank balance and saw a new deposit. For a split second, there's a flicker. A tiny spark of relief, maybe a little joy. Then, almost immediately, the "buts" start rolling in. "It's not enough." "I'll just spend it on bills anyway." "I don't deserve this." "It'll be gone before I know it." The shoulders slump a little. The jaw tightens. The breath hitches. That initial spark of receiving, that moment of energetic expansion, gets choked off before it can even fully bloom. Sound familiar?

Truth is, you're not alone. Most of us have a deeply ingrained, often unconscious, resistance to fully receiving. And when it comes to money, that resistance becomes a full-blown barricade. We talk a lot about earning money, managing money, saving money, even giving money. But how often do we talk about the pure, unadulterated act of receiving money? Not just intellectually, but somatically? In your body? Almost never. And that's a huge problem, because Mind Over Money (paid link) isn't just a clever title; it's a fundamental truth. Your relationship with money isn't just in your head; it's in your bones, your breath, your gut.

Here's the thing: your ability to receive money isn't just about your external circumstances or your financial literacy. It's about your internal world. It's about your nervous system. It's about how safe you feel, deep down, to open up and let abundance flow in. We've been conditioned to believe that receiving is passive, even weak. We're taught to strive, to hustle, to earn. And while effort is important, the act of receiving requires a different kind of strength - the strength of vulnerability, of trust, of surrender. Brené Brown's work on shame and vulnerability couldn't be more relevant here. We often feel shame around money, whether it's having too little, having too much, or even just the messy reality of our financial lives. This shame makes us contract, makes us close off, makes us unable to fully open our hands and our hearts to what's coming our way.

So, let's get real. The money wound runs deeper than your budget spreadsheet. It's woven into your personal history, your family's history, and the collective consciousness around wealth and worth. It manifests as physical tension, as a racing heart, as that familiar knot in your stomach whenever a significant financial transaction occurs. Your body remembers every financial stressor, every perceived lack, every moment of fear. And until you address that somatic memory, you'll always be pushing against an invisible wall when it comes to receiving.

The Body Keeps the Score: Your Nervous System and Your Net Worth

Think about it. When you're stressed, what happens to your body? Your shoulders hunch, your jaw clenches, your breath becomes shallow. This is your nervous system in fight-or-flight mode. Now, what happens when you're about to receive money, especially a larger sum, or money that feels "unearned"? Often, the same physiological response. It's paradoxical, isn't it? We want money, we work for money, we pray for money. But when it actually shows up, sometimes our bodies react as if it's a threat. Why? Because for many, money carries a heavy emotional load: fear of loss, fear of judgment, fear of responsibility, fear of not being enough, or fear of being "too much."

This isn't just woo-woo. It's basic biology. Your nervous system doesn't differentiate between a saber-toothed tiger and a large tax bill. It just registers stress. And when your body is in a state of chronic stress, it's not open to receiving. It's in survival mode, contracted and guarded. How can you expect abundance to flow into a closed system? You can't. It's like trying to pour water into a clenched fist. Some might splash on the outside, but very little gets in. Barbara Huson, in her work on overcoming underearning, talks about the internal barriers that keep women, in particular, from fully valuing their work and receiving what they're worth. These barriers aren't just mental; they're deeply embodied. They're the subtle ways we hold ourselves back, the unconscious signals we send that say, "I'm not quite ready for that much."

The somatic practice of receiving money is about retraining your nervous system. It's about creating a felt sense of safety and openness around money. It's about teaching your body that receiving is not a threat, but a natural, welcome flow. It's about dismantling those internal barricades, one breath at a time. It's a conscious, deliberate effort to align your physical self with your desire for financial well-being. Notice what your body does when you think about your debt. Does it tense up? Does your stomach churn? That's the somatic memory of scarcity playing out. We need to flip that script, not just in our minds, but in our very cells.

Beyond the Mind: Engaging Your Senses in Receiving

You can't just think your way into receiving more. You have to feel your way into it. This means engaging all your senses. When money comes in, what do you typically do? You look at the number on a screen. Maybe you get a notification. It's often a very cerebral, abstract experience. But what if you brought it down into your body?

Try this: When a payment comes in, or you look at a positive bank balance, pause. Don't immediately jump to the "what next" or the "it's not enough." Instead, close your eyes for a moment. Take a deep breath. Bring your attention to your physical body. Where do you feel this money? Is there a lightness in your chest? A warmth in your belly? A tingling in your hands? Even if it's just a tiny shift, acknowledge it. Allow yourself to feel that sensation without judgment. Let it expand a little. This isn't about being greedy or materialistic; it's about acknowledging a positive energetic exchange. It's about saying "yes" to the flow. Vicki Robin, in Radical Acceptance (paid link), talks about how we often resist the present moment, how we push away what is. This applies to money too. We resist the reality of what we have, always looking for more, always focusing on lack. But radical acceptance means accepting the money that's here, now, in this moment, in your body.

Another practice: When you physically touch money - a crisp bill, a handful of coins - really feel it. What's the texture? The weight? The smell? Engage your senses. This reconnects you to the tangible reality of money, making it less abstract and more real, more present. Most of our money is digital these days, which further disconnects us from its physical presence. Bringing it back to the senses helps ground the experience of receiving. You feel that? That's your body beginning to re-learn that money is not inherently scary or bad. It's simply energy, and you're allowing yourself to be a conduit for it.

The Art of the Open Hand: Releasing the Clench

Metaphorically and literally, many of us approach money with a clenched fist. We grab it, we hold onto it tightly, fearing it will slip away. This clenching, this gripping, is a physical manifestation of fear and scarcity. It's a protective mechanism. But it also prevents anything new from coming in. You can't receive with a closed hand. It's impossible. Right?!

The practice of the open hand is about releasing that clench. It's about consciously relaxing your body, particularly your hands, your jaw, your shoulders, your belly. When you receive money, whether it's a direct deposit or a physical check, take a moment to intentionally relax these areas. Imagine your hands are open, palms facing up, ready to receive. This isn't about being careless with your money; it's about being open to its flow. It's a gesture of trust, both in the universe and in your own ability to manage what comes your way. Sadhguru often speaks about the importance of being open to life, to experience, to the flow of existence. This principle extends directly to money. If you're constantly guarding, constantly protecting, you're not truly living, and you're certainly not truly receiving.

This isn't about magical thinking. It's about energetic alignment. When your body is relaxed and open, you're sending a different signal to the universe - and to your own subconscious. You're saying, "I am safe. I am ready. I am capable." This shift in internal state can absolutely impact your external reality, not because money just magically appears, but because your openness makes you more receptive to opportunities, more confident in your negotiations, and more aligned with your own worth. Your bank account is a mirror, not a scorecard. And if that mirror is reflecting a clenched, fear-driven energy, you'll find yourself constantly struggling against the current.

Facing the Shadow: Shame, Guilt, and Unworthiness

Honestly, a huge part of why we struggle to receive money somatically is because of the deep-seated shame, guilt, and feelings of unworthiness that surround it. "Who am I to have this much?" "I didn't work hard enough for this." "Someone else deserves it more." These are the insidious whispers of the money wound. These thoughts don't just float around in your head; they manifest as physical sensations. Guilt might feel like a heavy weight on your chest. Shame might feel like a hot flush, or a desire to shrink and disappear. Unworthiness might feel like a hollowness in your gut.

Brené Brown's research, particularly in The Gifts of Imperfection (paid link), illuminates how deeply these emotions impact our ability to live a wholehearted life. And a wholehearted life absolutely includes a healthy, open relationship with money. You can't be wholehearted and simultaneously shut off to financial abundance. It just doesn't work. To truly receive, you have to be willing to feel these uncomfortable emotions, acknowledge them, and then gently release them. You have to be willing to look at the stories you've internalized about money, worthiness, and your place in the economic world.

This isn't about self-flagellation. It's about compassionate awareness. When you notice that familiar feeling of shame or guilt creeping in when money arrives, instead of pushing it away, invite it in. Sit with it for a moment. Ask it, "What are you trying to tell me?" Often, these feelings are old protectors, trying to keep you safe from perceived dangers of having money (like being judged, exploited, or losing it all). Once you acknowledge them, you can gently reassure them that you're safe, that you're capable, and that it's okay to receive. This is a real act of self-compassion, and it's essential for truly opening your receiving channels. Know what I mean? It's a deep dive into your own psyche, and it’s worth it.

Practical Somatic Practices for Receiving

So, how do you actually do this? How do you move from intellectual understanding to embodied practice? Here are some concrete ways to integrate somatic receiving into your daily life:

  1. The Receiving Breath: When money comes in (a payment, a gift, even finding a coin), pause. Close your eyes. Take a deep, slow inhale, imagining you're breathing in the energy of abundance, of receiving. As you exhale, imagine releasing any tension, any resistance, any fear or unworthiness. Do this 3-5 times. Let the breath be your anchor to the present moment and to the act of receiving.
  2. The Open Hand Meditation: Sit comfortably. Place your hands in your lap, palms facing up, fingers gently curled. Imagine a warm, golden light (representing money, abundance, worthiness) slowly filling your hands. Feel its warmth, its weight. Allow your whole body to soften and open to this sensation. Stay with it for a few minutes. This simple practice reprograms your body's association with receiving.
  3. Sensory Engagement with Physical Money: If you use cash, don't just shove it in your wallet. When you receive change, look at the coins. Feel their texture. When you get a bill, notice its crispness. If you have a physical check, hold it for a moment. This tactile connection helps ground the energy of money in your physical reality.
  4. Gratitude and Acknowledgment: After receiving money, take a moment to genuinely say "thank you." Not just to the universe, but to yourself, to your work, to the source. Feel the gratitude in your body - perhaps a warmth in your heart, a lightness in your chest. This amplifies the positive somatic experience of receiving.
  5. Body Scan for Resistance: Whenever you're dealing with money (paying bills, checking balances, making financial decisions), do a quick body scan. Where are you holding tension? Is your jaw clenched? Shoulders tight? Stomach knotted? Consciously relax those areas. Breathe into them. This simple awareness can prevent you from shutting down your receiving channels.
  6. Visualize Receiving with Joy: Before a payment is due, or when you're setting a financial goal, visualize the money arriving. But don't just see the number. See yourself reacting to it with genuine joy, with an open heart, with a relaxed body. Feel the expansion, the relief, the gratitude. This pre-paves the neural pathways for a positive receiving experience.

This isn't about ignoring financial realities or pretending everything is fine. It's about creating an internal environment that is conducive to attracting and holding wealth, even in challenging times. You're teaching your body that money is a safe, welcome guest, not a terrifying intruder. Does that land? It should. Because your body has been waiting for this permission to relax around money for a long, long time.

Embracing the Flow: It's Not Just About Money

This somatic practice of receiving isn't limited to money. It's a practice for life. How well do you receive compliments? How well do you receive love, support, help? Often, our resistance to receiving money is just one facet of a broader resistance to receiving all forms of abundance and support. If you struggle to let someone help you, or to truly take in a heartfelt compliment, chances are you'll struggle to fully receive money too. It's all connected.

Sadhguru talks about the importance of making yourself available to life, of being a receptive channel. If you're constantly pushing, constantly striving, constantly guarded, you're not truly available. You're creating friction. The somatic work around money is ultimately about becoming more available to life itself, in all its forms. It's about trusting the flow, not just economically, but existentially. It's about realizing that you are inherently worthy of good things, including financial well-being, simply because you exist. This isn't about entitlement; it's about inherent worth. You don't have to earn your right to receive. You just have to open your hands.

So, the next time money comes your way, big or small, pause. Breathe. Feel it in your body. Let it land. And know that with every conscious act of receiving, you're not just growing your bank account, you're healing a deeper wound, and you're opening yourself up to a richer, more expansive life. This isn't a quick fix; it's a lifelong practice. But it's one that promises real returns, far beyond the purely financial.

What would it feel like to truly, deeply, somatically receive without reservation? How does that make you feel?

FAQ

Q: Is this just positive thinking?

A: No, it's not just positive thinking. While a positive mindset can be helpful, somatic receiving goes deeper. It addresses the physical and neurological patterns in your body that often override conscious thought. You can think positively all you want, but if your nervous system is still in a state of fear or contraction around money, you won't fully receive. This practice works to retrain your body's unconscious reactions.

Q: Does this mean I don't have to work hard for money anymore?

A: Absolutely not. This practice complements, rather than replaces, conscious effort and smart financial choices. It's about aligning your internal state with your external actions. When you're somatically open to receiving, you're likely to be more effective in your work, more confident in your negotiations, and more attuned to opportunities. It enhances your ability to earn and manage, it doesn't negate it.

Q: What if I have a lot of debt? Can I still practice somatic receiving?

A: Yes, especially then. The shame and stress associated with debt can create real physical contraction. Practicing somatic receiving, even with small amounts of money coming in or just by visualizing, can begin to shift that internal state. It helps to create a sense of safety and possibility, even when facing financial challenges. It's about creating space for relief and eventual resolution, rather than staying stuck in the fear and contraction of debt.

Om Shanti Shanti Shanti
meta_description: Learn to fully receive money without the "buts." This somatic practice helps you acknowledge and embody financial abundance, fostering a healthier relationship
author: Kalesh
